layout_description:
Option: OPT-1-REVISED
Slide description: SINGLE-CONTAINER FORMAL TABLE LAYOUT with maximized vertical fill. The slide uses a top-to-bottom orientation with the title occupying the top 12% of slide height and a single full-width table container filling the remaining 88% of vertical space. The table is a formal regulatory/legal data structure presenting tax categories, rates, applicable activities, and legal basis in a four-column grid. The design follows the Flat Geometry Table pattern (Section 4 Option C, design guidelines) — the mandatory choice for formal/regulatory/legal/financial content per Rule J2. The table features no filled header bars, small accent tabs beneath column headers, light grey row label cells in the first column, and thin dashed grey separator lines between rows. The layout maximizes vertical space utilization by expanding row heights to fill the container, addressing the judge's critical feedback that the previous attempt left more than half the container empty. Row heights are now distributed generously using equal flexGrow values so that the four data rows collectively occupy approximately 75% of the container's inner height (the remaining 25% is allocated to the header section and internal padding). Font sizes are increased to medium-to-large body text (target range: 16-18pt equivalent) to proportionally fill the expanded row cells. The table spans approximately 85% of the slide width, horizontally centered with equal left/right margins. All column edges align to an invisible 4-column grid. Row separators are positioned at consistent intervals between row bands, creating visual rhythm appropriate for formal regulatory content. Generous internal padding on all sides prevents content from crowding container edges, consistent with the sparse content density (4 data rows, moderate text length per cell).

Blueprint:
  Orientation: top-to-bottom
  Regions: 2
  Allocation: [0.12, 0.88]

Containers:
  C0 (FULL-WIDTH FORMAL TAX TABLE): This container holds a four-column regulatory table presenting tax categories for virtual asset activities. The table uses the Flat Geometry Table design pattern (Section 4 Option C) — the mandatory choice for formal/regulatory/legal content per Rule J2. ADDRESSING JUDGE FEEDBACK: The previous attempt left more than half the container empty below the last row. This revision increases row heights significantly by using larger flexGrow values for each data row, increases font size to medium-to-large body text (target: 16-18pt equivalent), and increases vertical padding within cells. The four data rows now collectively fill approximately 75% of the container's inner vertical space, with the header section and internal padding occupying the remaining 25%. This ensures the container fill ratio exceeds 65% as required. STRUCTURE: Four equal-width columns arranged left to right: Tax Type (25% width) | Rate (25% width) | Applicable Activity (25% width) | Legal Basis (25% width). Columns are visually separated by alignment and content, not by physical gutters — no gaps between columns. COLUMN HEADERS: Plain bold text in heading font, brand dark color, left-aligned within each column, positioned at the top of the table with a small top padding (approximately 3% of container height). No filled header bar. Below each header label sits a small accent tab: brand primary color fill, very thin in height (approximately 2-3% of container height), narrow width (approximately 12-15% of column width), top corners slightly rounded, bottom edge square, left-aligned directly beneath the header text, sitting fully below the text with no overlap. Immediately below and touching each accent tab is a thin solid horizontal line in brand dark color, spanning that column's full width. The four header lines sit at the same vertical position but do not connect across columns — each is confined to its column width with small white gutters (approximately 1% of table width) between the line endpoints. A small vertical gap (approximately 2% of container height) separates the header section from the first data row. DATA ROWS: Four data rows distributed evenly across the remaining vertical space using equal flexGrow values. Each row occupies approximately 18-20% of the container height (significantly expanded from the previous attempt). Row content: Row 1 = Income Tax | 10-35% | Mining, staking, airdrops, crypto payments | Section 3, Income Tax Act. Row 2 = Capital Gains | 15% | Gains on disposal of virtual assets | Capital Gains Tax Act. Row 3 = Excise Duty | 10% | Fees charged by exchanges & service providers | Finance Act 2025. Row 4 = Withholding Tax | Various | Payments to non-residents for crypto services | Income Tax Act. FIRST COLUMN (Tax Type) TREATMENT: Each cell in the first column is a light grey filled rectangle (light grey alt color from palette, no border, no rounded corners, spanning the full column width and approximately 80% of the row height, vertically centered within the row band) with bold text in brand dark color, medium-to-large body font size (target: 16-18pt equivalent), left-aligned with a small left inset (approximately 3% of column width), vertically centered within the grey rectangle. The grey fill visually distinguishes the row labels as the primary identifier for each row. OTHER COLUMNS (Rate, Applicable Activity, Legal Basis): Plain body text in body font, medium-to-large size (same as first column: 16-18pt equivalent), brand text color, left-aligned with a small left inset (approximately 3% of column width), vertically centered within the row band. No background fill — white/transparent. The Rate column uses bold text (rates are key data points); the Applicable Activity and Legal Basis columns use regular weight. Text wraps naturally within the column width. Font size is set to fill the expanded row height proportionally — the increased row height from the judge feedback allows for larger, more readable text. Vertical padding within each cell is generous (approximately 3-4% of row height on top and bottom) to create breathing room around the text and prevent it from crowding the row edges. ROW SEPARATORS: Thin dashed grey horizontal lines (light grey color, dashed stroke pattern, thin weight) between consecutive rows: between Row 1 and Row 2, between Row 2 and Row 3, between Row 3 and Row 4. No separator after Row 4. Each separator spans the full table width from the left edge of the first column to the right edge of the fourth column. Separators are positioned at the exact boundary between row bands — they sit at the midpoint between the bottom edge of one row's content area and the top edge of the next row's content area. Because rows are now generously sized, the separators have visible space above and below them (approximately 1-2% of container height on each side), creating a balanced rhythm rather than sitting tight to the content. VISUAL HIERARCHY: The first column's light grey fill and bold text create clear visual weight, establishing the tax type as the primary row identifier. The Rate column's bold text signals key data. The accent tabs beneath column headers add a jewel-like formal detail without clutter. The generous row heights and increased font size ensure all text is comfortably readable and the table feels substantial rather than sparse. ALIGNMENT: All text in each column shares the same left edge (left-aligned with consistent inset). All row bands across all four columns share identical top and bottom edges — this is a hard constraint from row-group detection (Step 0.5c). The four row separators are perfectly horizontal and aligned across all columns. The accent tabs are left-aligned with their respective column headers. SPACING: The table has generous internal padding on all sides: approximately 4% of slide width on left and right edges, approximately 3% of container height on top edge (above column headers), and approximately 3% of container height on bottom edge (below the last row separator). This padding is appropriate for the sparse content density (four rows, moderate text length) and prevents content from crowding the container edges. The increased row heights and font sizes fill the vertical space between the top and bottom padding, eliminating the empty white space that was present in the previous attempt. CONTAINER BACKGROUND: White fill. No border (the table's internal structure provides sufficient visual definition through accent tabs, row separators, and grey-filled label cells). No rounded corners (flat geometry aesthetic per Section 4 Option C). No shadow or glow (formal regulatory tables do not use decorative effects per the design guidelines). The container fills the available vertical space between the title and the bottom slide margin, maximizing slide coverage while maintaining comfortable whitespace at the slide edges. FONT SIZE DECISION (addressing judge feedback): The previous attempt used a font size that was too small for the available row height, contributing to the under-filled appearance. This revision increases the font size to medium-to-large body text (target: 16-18pt equivalent) across all table cells. This is consistent with Rule C6 (within-slide font uniformity) — all body text in the table uses the same font size. The larger font size is proportional to the expanded row heights and ensures the text comfortably fills each cell without appearing cramped or wasteful. Per the font size decision framework (Rules C6/C7), this is Scenario 2 (Fill the box) — the container dimensions are fixed by the slide structure, and increasing the font size improves legibility and visual balance. The font increase is applied uniformly to all table body text, preserving within-slide consistency.
  SC-BOTTOM (DECORATIVE BACKGROUND LAYER): INACTIVE. The slide content (single table container) fills the available space sufficiently. The table container itself provides all necessary visual structure through its internal column headers, accent tabs, row separators, and grey-filled label cells. Adding a bottom decorative layer would serve no purpose — it would not group multiple containers (there is only one container), and it would not fill empty space (the revised table now fills the container vertically, addressing the judge's feedback that the previous attempt left excessive empty space). Per Rule D1, the bottom decorative layer is used ONLY when content does not fill the slide or when multiple containers need visual grouping. Neither condition applies here. The slide is well-filled by the table container alone, and the table's internal design provides sufficient visual richness through the Flat Geometry Table pattern.
  SC-TOP (OVERLAY CONNECTOR LAYER): INACTIVE. This slide contains a single table with no cross-container relationships, no process flow arrows, and no elements that span multiple containers. There are no connectors, annotation lines, or overlay elements required. Per Rule E3 (top-layer minimalism), the top layer is used only for elements that genuinely span containers or sit above all containers. Since this slide has only one container and no inter-container relationships, the top layer remains empty. All visual structure is contained within the table container itself.
